title: Compounding Growth

content.question: What compounding mechanism are you neglecting?
sop: List which of your current efforts pay off once (a single sale) vs. repeatedly (a referral system, evergreen content, recurring revenue)
Pick one compounding mechanism to build deliberately this quarter
Set the fixed input, rate, amount, or frequency you'll commit to (see calculation, page 6)
Reinvest the output back into the same mechanism instead of spending or abandoning it
Review growth quarterly, compounding is invisible week to week and obvious year to year
content.explainer: A system that compounds doesn't feel impressive in week one, the curve looks almost flat. What makes it different from a one-time effort is that the output gets reinvested as a new input, so growth builds on growth instead of starting over. The neglect isn't usually laziness; it's that compounding is boring long before it's obvious.
questions: What compounding mechanism are you neglecting?
What are you doing right now that produces a one-time result instead of a result that keeps paying you back?
If you kept doing today's smallest consistent action for three years straight, would the result actually be big, or just repetitive?

definitions.def: What an amount grows into after compounding over time.
The starting amount before any compounding is applied.

content.equation_example: PV: $1,000 starting input. r: 10% growth rate per period. n: 5 periods. FV = 1,000 times (1.10 to the 5th power) = $1,611. Higher r or more periods (n) means the same starting input turns into a dramatically bigger result the longer you let it run; a low r or stopping early means you're mostly just seeing the original input back.
content.closing_statement: Small, repeated, reinvested inputs beat big one-time pushes.

caption:
Principle Nº 212: Compounding Growth

Build systems that scale themselves. A system that compounds doesn't feel impressive in week one, the curve looks almost flat.

Ask yourself: What compounding mechanism are you neglecting?
